coredata.dc:title: Experimental investigation of explosion hazard from lithium-ion battery thermal runaway effluent gas

Gaudet, Benjamin

coredata.dc:description: Fire and explosion hazards present a serious concern to the widespread adoption of battery technology. This work experimentally investigates the explosion hazards associated with synthesized lithium-ion battery thermal runaway effluent gases (TREG) in an enclosed garage space typical of modern construction in North America. Pressure rise inside the compartment is examined using high-frequency piezoelectric pressure transducers. Data on overpressure and impulse is compared with known ranges for structural damage and bodily injury thresholds and calculation methods for maximum overpressure. Data on time-resolved overpressure are compared with a vented explosion model from literature. These comparisons support that existing models for maximum overpressure and time-resolved pressure rise remain valid for the examined synthesized TREG. Correlations are developed between gas volume and measured impulse and overpressure. An extension of this analysis with values of TREG production from literature supports that pressures measured in these experiments can be generated from ignition of gases released from batteries sized in the range of 3300W ⋅ h to 80 500W ⋅ h.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fire and explosion hazards present a serious concern to the widespread adoption of battery technology. This work experimentally investigates the explosion hazards associated with synthesized lithium-ion battery thermal runaway effluent gases (TREG) in an enclosed garage space typical of modern construction in North America. Pressure rise inside the compartment is examined using high-frequency piezoelectric pressure transducers. Data on overpressure and impulse is compared with known ranges for structural damage and bodily injury thresholds and calculation methods for maximum overpressure. Data on time-resolved overpressure are compared with a vented explosion model from literature. These comparisons support that existing models for maximum overpressure and time-resolved pressure rise remain valid for the examined synthesized TREG. Correlations are developed between gas volume and measured impulse and overpressure. An extension of this analysis with values of TREG production from literature supports that pressures measured in these experiments can be generated from ignition of gases released from batteries sized in the range of 3300W ⋅ h to 80 500W ⋅ h.

Data for Partial Volume Deflagration Experiments with Synthesized Lithium-Ion Battery Thermal Runaway Effluent Gas. (Original data) (Zenodo)
.

ce:section.ce:para: Battery-powered devices are becoming more commonplace in the home with their steady increase in affordability, applications, and practicality. The most common lithium-ion battery (LiB) powered devices in modern homes are electric vehicles (EV), battery energy storage systems (BESS), e-mobility devices such as e-scooters, and battery-powered tools. Thermal runaway of lithium-ion (Li-ion) batteries can be caused by manufacturing defects, thermal abuse, electrical abuse, mechanical abuse, ineffective battery management systems, and phenomena related to battery aging. If the failure causes cells to enter thermal runaway the resulting exothermic reactions generate gases inside the cell, which can then vent into the surroundings [1,2]. This vented gas, called thermal runaway effluent gases (TREG), is flammable and can accumulate in the surrounding space, precipitating an explosion hazard.

Several explosion events have occurred attributed to the ignition of gases released from the failure of Li-ion batteries inside enclosed spaces. One such incident occurred in Surprise, Arizona, when TREG accumulated inside of a Li-ion BESS enclosure. After emergency responders opened a man-door to conduct hazardous gas measurements, the accumulated gases ignited, causing an explosion. One member of the responding team was thrown over 21m (70ft) from the force of the venting gases. Injuries responders sustained during this incident required overnight hospitalization of four of the emergency responders [3–5]. In another incident in Erie, Colorado, flammable gases accumulated from the thermal runaway of a lithium-ion battery in a hybrid vehicle. While first responders were operating inside the structure, an ignition source triggered a deflagration inside the garage housing the vehicle. The roll-up garage door was ejected into the driveway, contacting the incident commander’s helmet but sparing serious injury [6,7]. In August 2023, the thermal runaway of an EV battery caused a severe explosion in a detached garage that knocked down masonry walls, destroyed adjacent car bays and damaged nearby homes [8]. These incidents demonstrate that a thorough understanding of the explosion hazards inherent to LiBTREG is needed to address the evolving safety considerations for LiB-powered products stored in or near residential structures.

Previous experimental works have developed data on the severity of flammable gas explosions. Blais [9], Sommersel [10], and Skjold [11] developed hazard data for hydrogen explosions in a residential garage and inter-modal containers. Mueschke and Joyce [12] conducted experiments in which premixed hydrogen gas bags were ignited in a reinforced blast chamber. Pressure sensors were used in the chamber and downfield to measure overpressure and impulse. These data were compared to the damage thresholds established by Baker et al. to assess the damage their experiments might incur on structures [12,13].

The accumulation of TREG in the presence of a competent ignition source poses a deflagration hazard and, if the gases are released into an enclosed space, an explosion hazard. Damage thresholds have been developed within the literature to inform structural design. Baker et al. [13] developed damage and injury thresholds caused by explosion blast waves by correlating catalogued damage to measured overpressure and impulse (P-I). The data and curve fits for these damage thresholds, initially established by Jarrett [14], addressed damage to brick homes in the United Kingdom during the Second World War. Modern works still compare data to these originally established thresholds for damage and injury. However, given the stark difference in construction since the development of Jarrett’s data, new pressure and damage assessment data are needed to assess explosion hazards in modern residential construction.

Correlations to predict compartment overpressure based on thermodynamic modeling of a partial volume deflagration have also been developed [15,16]. These models consider the maximum closed vessel deflagration pressure (
P
m a x
) of the flammable gas and volumes of the gas and enclosure. Although not time-resolved, these models estimate the maximum overpressure and are used in engineering applications such as the Standard on Explosion Protection by Deflagration Venting, NFPA 68 [17]. Models have also been developed for predicting overpressure and impulse in explosion events. These models can be used to support engineering analyses for explosion hazard mitigation. Foundational work on time-resolved pressure modeling of vented explosions was performed by Mulpuru and Wilkin [18] using a series of differential equations to describe pressure rise. Other works have modified this method to include flame shape, propagation velocity, enclosure properties, and obstacle effects [19]. To date, the efficacy of these models has not yet been demonstrated experimentally for TREG.

The composition and thermodynamic properties of TREG are key to evaluating the explosion hazard. They are also areas of ongoing research. It has been shown that the composition of gases released from an LiB in thermal runaway is related to, among other factors, cathode chemistry and state of charge (SOC). Golubkov et al. [20] measured TREG composition for 18650-format cells at 100% SOC finding 29.4% hydrogen and 48.3% carbon dioxide were measured in TREG from a lithium iron phosphate (LFP) cell. In comparison, 22.6–28.5% hydrogen and 17.5–22.7% carbon-dioxide concentration were measured in TREG across 3 trials of identical nickel cobalt aluminum (NCA) cells. In similar tests, Lammer et al. [21] studied the thermal abuse of NCA 18650 cells, and reported the resulting gas composition as hydrogen ( ≈ 32%), carbon monoxide ( ≈ 47%) and carbon dioxide ( ≈ 15%). Yuan et al. [22] examined the TREG composition of 26650 LFP cells in an accelerating rate calorimeter, measuring a composition of 24.3% hydrogen, 25.4% carbon dioxide, 4.5% carbon monoxide, 5.9% methane, 3.3% ethylene, 1.3% ethane and 0.08% acetylene. A similar study by Golubkov in 2014 studied LFP 18650 cells under thermal abuse and measured a composition of 30.9% hydrogen, 53.0% carbon dioxide, 4.8% carbon monoxide, 4.1% methane, 6.8% ethylene and 0.3% ethane [23]. These studies are examples of the indeterminate composition of TREG in literature, even across identical cell chemistries and formats in the same study. Some studies report significantly different concentrations, while others still report entire gas species absent from others.

Studies have been published that focus on the explosion hazard of TREG by experimentally examining properties such as the lower flammable limit (LFL) and upper flammable limit (UFL). Chen et al. [24] examined the LFL with varying states of charge and heating rates from thermally abused LFP and nickel cobalt manganese (NCM) 18650 cells. Zhang et al. [25] conducted similar work but examined the LFL and UFL behavior of NCM 18650 TREG gas in situ. Other studies have numerically investigated the explosion hazard of LiB TREG. Baird et al. [26] established that the Mulpuru model may apply to TREG. Baird first compared the pressure rise from hydrogen gas mixtures ignited in confined spaces to Mulpuru model outputs, then demonstrated that experimental results from partial fractions of hydrogen were comparable to pressure rise predicted from Mulpuru with simulated TREG inputs [26]. Peng et al. [27] numerically modeled the explosion hazards from TREG in containerized energy storage systems, finding that LiB TREG does pose a significant explosion hazard that could be mitigated with proper deflagration vent design considerations [27].

Large scale experimental investigation of explosion hazards from LiB TREG is limited in literature. Henriksen et al. [28] developed a computational fluid dynamics method for the simulation of a lithium-ion battery TREG explosion and compared these to experimental results in a 1000.0mm by 65.0mm by 116.5mm explosion channel. Henriksen primarily focuses on laminar burning velocity behavior and maximum pressure when comparing simulation to experimental behavior [28]. Mao et al. [29] experimentally investigated the deflagration behavior of lithium-ion battery TREG in a confined space under different ventilation conditions. Mao utilized arrangements of four 18650 batteries placed inside a 30.0cm by 30.0cm by 40.0cm stainless steel box, then forced into thermal runaway by thermal abuse [29]. The accumulation and ignition of TREG was found to be possible even on a small scale, measuring peak pressures of 0.2MPa to 1.2MPa at the top of the confinement. Empirical studies on the explosion hazards of TREG have been conducted on a small scale, and large-scale explosion hazard analyses have been simulation-based or have not included TREG or synthesized TREG. This study addresses the experimental gap in the literature by reporting large-scale empirical measurements of explosion damage, overpressure, and impulse caused by the ignition of synthesized TREG.

The objectives of the present study are to experimentally characterize the explosion hazards of accumulated TREG in an enclosed space and to evaluate the utility of explosion models to predict these explosion hazards. This includes predictions of overpressure and impulse, volume of burned gas, and structural damage. Synthetic gas mixtures (synTREG) corresponding to measured TREG values for NCA and LFP cathode chemistry Li-ion batteries were produced. Test scenarios included premixed partial volumes and controlled releases of these synthetic mixtures in an attached residential two-car garage space. The flammable gas volumes in each test represented fractions of the lower flammable limit (LFL) when considering the volume of the entire space. The synTREG volumes were ignited, and the pressure–time history was measured.

Identifying and examining the explosion hazards of TREG being released into an enclosed space was accomplished with two primary series of experiments. Both series utilized a residential two-car garage structure as the enclosure (Section 2.1). Rather than attempting to reliably fail batteries and test the effluent gases themselves, mixtures of gases representing TREG were used in situ. The composition and explosibility properties of LiB TREG assumed in this work were measured in controlled experiments, detailed in Section 2.4, to enable the creation of a synthetic TREG representing LiB TREG. When referencing this synthetic mixture, the term ‘synTREG’ is used throughout. Two types of synTREG were used, representing NCA and LFP battery chemistries (Section 2.4).

In the first experimental series, denoted as ‘gas bag’ experiments, pre-fabricated 0.06mm thick polyethylene bags were filled with synTREG and air at an equivalence ratio coinciding with the maximum burning velocity, determined by analysis of TREG from lithium-ion batteries. The bag size was selected such that when the target amount of gas mixture was added, the bag was fully inflated. This test series was designed to determine the explosion damage potential from the release and ignition of TREG in a limited fraction of a larger volume. In these scenarios, called partial volume deflagrations, the total volume of TREG released may be lower than what is deemed ignitable if that gas were uniformly mixed in the full volume. This assumption, called ‘well-mixed’, is made in some explosion analysis methods [17]. In these methods, 25% of the LFL is used to assess explosion hazards. These experiments address the hypothesis that relatively small volumes of TREG have the potential for significant damage when released into a larger space. Furthermore, since the mixture concentration of gases is well controlled, these data are more suitable for evaluating explosion models for assessing the explosion hazards of TREG.

The second experimental series, denoted as ‘dispersion’ experiments, involved releasing larger amounts of flammable gas at a single location in the enclosure and allowing the gases to disperse through the space before ignition. These experiments simulated a single-point release of TREG into a space, analogous to thermal runaway events. SynTREG was released at 100% concentration into a metal housing, representing a wall-mounted BESS. The target gas volumes for these experiments were the amounts needed for full-volume concentrations equivalent to 80% of the LFL of the NCA surrogate gas mixture and 100% of the LFL of the LFP surrogate gas mixture. Dispersion experiments are more representative of real-world TREG release scenarios but are less comparable to mathematical models, as gas mixing and dispersion are not spatially uniform. Knowledge of the flammable gas concentration at a point in space is needed to accurately designate explosibility parameters such as
P
m a x
and
S
L
. This makes applying time-resolved pressure models more reliant on additional assumptions and introduces potentially compounding uncertainties.

Experiments were performed at an outdoor explosives test site in western Illinois between September and October 2022. The outdoor temperature during experiments was 21°C ± 8°C. Earth berms ranging from 9m to 15m tall sheltered the test area from prevailing winds; typical wind speeds were less than 5m ⋅ s−1.

The enclosure (113.5m3) was designed to represent an attached residential two-car garage. The garage is a typical location of residential energy storage systems, electric vehicles, and battery-powered tools; and is the likely location where TREG may accumulate in residential structures. This research effort followed a U.S.Department of Energy project that demonstrated that releases of TREG from a residential energy storage system could create deflagration hazards [30]. In that project, U.S.Census Bureau data was analyzed and it was determined that the most common North American home built in the last 40 years is typically constructed as a ‘tract home’, which includes an attached two-car garage [31]. This style of construction employs lightweight platform construction combined with wood stud framing.

Walls were constructed with nominally 5.1cm (2in) by 12.7cm (4in) SPF #2 wooden studs spaced 61cm (24in) from center to center, with vinyl siding over 1.3cm (0.5in) thick fiberboard and 1.6cm (0.625in) thick Type X gypsum wallboard on the interior. Wallboard seams were sealed with paper tape and joint compound. Exterior corners utilized 1.3cm (0.5in) thick plywood instead of fiberboard. The interior ceiling utilized 1.6cm (0.625in) Type X gypsum board, and no surfaces were painted. Adjacent rooms were constructed on the rear and to one side of the garage to represent the structural reinforcement normally provided by the remainder of a tract home. The structure was anchored to a concrete pad. A 4.9m (16ft) wide by 2.1m (7ft) tall steel overhead door was installed at the front of the garage, and a 76cm (30in) wide by 203cm (80in) tall steel-over-polystyrene core 20-minute fire-rated interior door was installed in the center of the opposite wall. Fig. 1 shows photographs of the exterior and interior of the garage space.

Flush-mount PCB Piezotronics high-frequency ICP® 113B28 piezoelectric pressure transducers connected to a 481 A PCB signal conditioner using 003C10 low-noise coaxial cable were used for measuring time-resolved pressure. These sensors utilize a quartz piezoelectric element, have a measurement range of 344.7kPa, sensitivity ±14.5mV ⋅ kPa−1, rise time ≤ 1.0µs and a resolution of 0.007kPa [32]. Five of these sensors were placed inside the garage enclosure: one on each of the four walls and one on the ceiling. Locations of these sensors in both the bag and dispersion experiments are presented in Figs. 2 and 3. Pressure data was recorded at 20kHz with a Yokogawa DL950 data acquisition recorder. It was determined that a 5kHz data rate was sufficient to resolve changes in pressure with time while reducing computational cost. As a first step, the data was reduced from 20kHz to 5kHz. Data recording was triggered simultaneously with the ignition equipment (described below). Data were acquired one second before and nine seconds after the ignition trigger.

In the dispersion experiments, four portable methane gas detectors (ATO model GD200-CH4) measured methane concentration at three locations along the ceiling and one central location inside the garage enclosure (Figs. 2 and 3). These devices utilized a built-in diaphragm pump and non-dispersive infrared gas sensor calibrated for methane to measure and record methane concentration at 0.2kHz. During the analysis of these data, it was assumed that the relative concentrations of gas species in the flammable gas composition stayed constant as the mixture dispersed and entrained air. It was also assumed that the methane measurement was a suitable proxy to infer the volume fraction of the overall flammable gas mixture. Methane concentration was used rather than hydrogen or carbon monoxide as portable methane gas sensors were cost-effective and readily available, making methane the most practical option.

Two GoPro Hero 8 video cameras recording at 240fps were placed inside the garage. One camera was located on the B-wall facing the D-wall, and the second on the C-wall facing the garage door. Video recordings were used for qualitative insight into the effects of overpressure on the structure and the garage door. A Photron FASTCAM Mini AX200 was used to capture high-speed images at 1024px
×
1024px resolution and 3000fps of the gas bag during ignition and fireball growth. This high-speed camera was only utilized in the 165L and 330L NCA gas bag experiments.

The controlled mixing of the synTREG with air and transport to the test enclosure was accomplished with the same system for the bag and dispersion experiments shown in Fig. 4. NCA and LFP synTREG were pre-formulated in the mixture concentrations in Table 1 and stored in pressurized bottles at 13.8MPa. Pressure in the fuel control line was reduced to 0.517MPa via a two-stage regulator at the synTREG supply bottle. These flammable gas compositions were mixed with air to achieve the desired mixture stoichiometry for a given test. The synTREG concentration was selected for bag tests to achieve the maximum flame speed determined to be 37.5% ( φ
=
1.54) for NCA mixtures and 28.4% ( φ
=
1.33) for LFP mixtures. Equivalence ratio ( φ ) refers to the ratio of the mass of fuel and air present in the mixture divided by the ratio of the mass of fuel to air in the stoichiometric condition.

Each bag was positioned against the D-side interior wall shown in Fig. 2. The bags were suspended at the center height of the interior wall surface, with the centroid of the bag located one radius away from the wall. The igniter was centered inside the bag volume. Ignition in the bag experiments was accomplished with a single-point pyrotechnic initiator (MJG Technologies Firewire).

Bags were filled and recirculated with an explosion-proof blower until a target methane volume concentration was measured. The methane concentration was used to measure the synTREG concentration, assuming uniform composition. Methane concentration was measured using a non-dispersive infrared gas sensor (Teledyne Gasurveyor 700). This constituted a check of the actual synTREG volume by bag filling confirmation, timed flow through a mass flow controller (Alicat MCR-1000LPM-D-DB15/CM) with an accuracy of ±(0.8% of reading +0.2% of full scale), and target methane concentration inside of the bag. Sixty seconds before ignition, mixing was terminated to allow gas motion in the bag to quiesce.

For the dispersion experiments, the synTREG was released through a 2.5cm (1in) tube into the center of a simulated residential ESS product enclosure of dimensions 0.41m (16.1in) wide by 1.35m (53.1in) tall by 0.08m (3.1in) thick centered horizontally on the D-side wall of the garage. SynTREG was introduced at 100% fuel concentration and at a rate of 572L ⋅ min−1. Mixing with room air occurred when synTREG leaked from a 0.32cm (0.13in) gap around the cover, and two 3.8cm (1.5in) holes simulated electronics cooling fan ports near the top of the housing. Ignition in these experiments was accomplished with an electrically initiated chemical pyrotechnic device (Le Maitre Gerb) that generated a shower of sparks.

Stratification of the released flammable gases is not believed to have played a significant role in explosibility properties at ignition. A barometric treatment of Boltzmann’s distribution of energy by Azatyan et al. [33] demonstrated that gravitational stratification of species from a gas mixture requires a large difference in molecular weights of component gases over a considerable height and an absence of any convection currents. Azatyan demonstrated a less than 0.2% change in the ratio of the number of hydrogen molecules to inhibitor molecules over a 1.5m height [33].

Synthetic gas mixtures representative of TREG for NCA and LFP cathode chemistries (synTREG) were used in place of TREG generated from failed batteries. As a first step, the composition of TREG from lithium-ion batteries in thermal runaway was determined from the same method detailed by Barowy et al. [34]. As in this preceding work, 18650 NCA and LFP cells were heated until thermal runaway using externally applied heaters within a pressure vessel with an inert nitrogen atmosphere. The TREG was collected and then analyzed in a gas chromatograph-mass spectrometer (GC-MS) to discern component gases. A synthesized gas mixture was created of the top four major gas species measured by GC-MS: hydrogen, carbon monoxide, carbon dioxide, and methane. The total hydrocarbon content of the TREG was included in the methane component. The composition of the species in each gas varied as summarized in Table 1. Between NCA and LFP TREG the fractions of hydrogen and methane are significantly different, which can be a driving factor for explosibility properties.

One limitation of formulating a gas mixture representing LiB TREG is that some minor component gases and vapors vented during thermal runaway may condense out of the mixture before measurement or within the synTREG compressed gas cylinders. If using these components, it would be necessary to heat the compressed gas cylinder to drive them into the gas phase. Once released, these components may recondense if not sufficiently heated during experiments. Additionally, this analysis did not measure or include potentially unquantified particulates and aerosols emitted during thermal runaway venting. It should be acknowledged that these additional gases and particulates could impact explosibility properties, however present literature is inconclusive about the quantities and degrees of these contributions. To improve repeatability, the synTREG in this study consisted of only the major TREG components that are in the gas phase at temperatures and pressures needed to transport them in compressed gas cylinders (typically room temperature 20°C, and approximately 1.5
× 1
0
4
kPa). Fractions of other gases measured in the composition of LiB TREG of other studies, such as acetylene, ethane, and ethylene, were left out of the synTREG composition to simplify its composition and focus on the consequences of the explosion hazard while additionally benefitting from easier manufacture and management.

A total of 12 experiments are analyzed in this study, including 10 gas bag experiments and two dispersion experiments. The key variables in each experiment are provided in Table 2. In this table, ‘%LFL’ and ‘ φ ’ represent the percent of the LFL and the equivalence ratio calculated for a well-mixed assumption in the complete garage space volume. This is denoted on Table 2 by the enclosure subscript: (%LFLenc and φ
enc). In the later sections, supplemental analysis is conducted on five experiments to model time-resolved pressure and damage to the structure. The experiments chosen for this analysis were cases in which the garage door had sustained serious damage or had become dislodged and ejected from the garage. A summary of all experiments is given in Table 2, experiments modeled later are emphasized in the first column.

Two works are commonly used for estimating maximum enclosure pressure dependent on flammable gas release volumes: Ogle [15] and Jo and Park [16]. The first model, the adiabatic mixing explosion model (AMEM), assumes an initial state of a sub-volume of unburned flammable mixture within a larger enclosure volume. The model then assumes a final state of adiabatic, isochoric mixing of the burned combustion products of this flammable mixture with the surrounding ambient air of the larger volume. The stoichiometric maximum closed vessel deflagration pressure (
P
m a x
) is scaled between the initial and final states to generate an explosion pressure within the larger enclosure. The second model, called the Gaussian distribution explosion model (GDEM), uses the same base assumptions except that (1) the fuel concentration follows a Gaussian distribution along its radius and (2) only the concentrations of the sub-volume within the flammable range contribute to pressure rise. In the present work, each method was used to calculate overpressures assuming volumes of flammable gas examined in the bag experiments. The model results were then compared to the experimentally measured overpressure to examine the effectiveness of these relatively simple models at estimating overpressure for synTREG events.

Several modeling approaches exist to simulate pressure rise with time for ventilated enclosure explosions. The model of focus was developed by Mulpuru and Wilkin in 1982 and has since been adapted into other works calculating pressure rise with time in ventilated explosions [18,26]. This model simultaneously solves a system of ordinary differential equations consisting of the conservation of mass, conservation of energy, and rate of burned gas production. The assumptions include point-source ignition, spherical flame growth, that burned and unburned gases act ideally, isentropic compression and expansion of gases, gas properties and pressure are spatially uniform, and that acoustic effects and compartment obstacles are neglected. Experimentally determined (Fig. 6) constant flame speed was applied to the model. Details on the derivation of the system of equations are available in Rangwala and Zalosh [38]. The final forms of the equations, as given in Ugarte et al. [19] and Rangwala and Zalosh [38], are presented in Eqs. (1), (2), (3), and (4). This system was solved numerically using Mathematica14 from ignition to the time of maximum experimental overpressure measured in the compartment. The thermodynamic equilibrium software GasEQ0.79 [39], assuming an adiabatic isochoric condition, was used to calculate the unburned and burned gas properties for use in the model.

A final step in applying the Mulpuru model to experimental data was the assumption of zero vent area. When evaluating the model against experimental data, pressure rise analysis was confined to the time preceding the garage door being majorly damaged or ejected. This simplifies the system as terms related to conserving vented mass fraction reduce to zero. Two NCA cases were chosen for model evaluation across the same gas composition and equivalence ratio but with differing volumes. An LFP case was also conducted to observe behavior across gas compositions.

For comparison to the time-resolved vented explosion (Mulpuru) model, the time-resolved volume of burned gas was measured for the 165L and 330L NCA gas bag experiments. These cases were the two largest volume NCA cases for which high-speed camera footage of the ignition and growth of the flammable gas cloud was recorded. Video frames were analyzed using the Python packages tkinter (a standard Python package [40]) and imageIO [41] by adjusting the pixel scaling and image cropping factors. OpenCV [42] was used to greyscale the image, apply a Gaussian blur and a binarization, and identify contours assuming a minimal enclosing circle defined by the largest contour. Binarization threshold values between 127 and 160 were optimal for this video analysis, but vary depending on average image brightness and flame front luminance. The volume of the growing fireball was then calculated for each frame using the radius of the fit circle and assuming a spherical volume. Stored values for time, volume, and radius were then exported. Examples of the exported video frames showing the contour-fit circle and flame shape evolution over time are shown in Fig. 5.

Measurements of
S
L
and
P
m a x
versus equivalence ratio for the two synTREG cases are presented in Fig. 6. Sixth-order polynomial functions were fit to each set of experimental data; fit parameters were calculated by least-squares regression, and coefficients of determination are provided in the figure.

The LFP flame speed case aside, all cases presented coefficients of determination greater than 0.92 between the curve fit and data. The LFP flame speed case had a coefficient of determination of 0.64, a significantly poorer agreement between the curve fit and experimental results than the other cases. In this case, the
S
L
measured at approximately φ
=
1.4 experienced high variance due to the high hydrogen content causing flame propagation instability resulting in measurement variance and a low coefficient of determination.

Throughout the experiment series, cosmetic and structural damage to the structure occurred which was repaired between experiments; Fig. 7 displays the types of damage observed during testing. These damages, even if not structurally critical, compromised the fire resistance of the wall and ceiling assemblies between the garage and the attached home. In the event of a prolonged fire post-deflagration, the designed fire resistance of the garage space would no longer prevent spread into the residential space as intended. Cracking of the sealed joints between drywall panels was observed in almost all experiments.

The top row of Fig. 7 shows damage incurred during the 320L LFP bag experiment. This experiment indicated a lower threshold of garage door ejection. In this case, the top corner of the garage door was pushed outward, but one side of the door remained attached. Also observed in this experiment was slight deformation to the man-door latch plate and cracking of drywall joints. The middle row of Fig. 7 shows damage resultant from the 826L NCA gas experiment, where the door was completely ejected from the garage. Similar damage to the man-door latch plate as the 320L LFP experiment was observed. This test observed significant damage, producing penetrating gaps between adjacent drywall sheets. The bottom row of Fig. 7 shows the 1651L NCA bag case, the experiment with the highest maximum internal overpressure measured. In this experiment, the garage door was ejected from the structure, wooden structural members cracked, the man-door latch plate was destroyed, and drywall panels were demolished and dislodged from the wall. Cracking and splitting of drywall joints were observed at the lowest bag volumes (83L), example images are shown in Fig. 7. More significant drywall damage was observed during experiments ≥ 826L NCA gas, where joints were destroyed, and penetrating gaps were made between adjacent panels. Most major structure members were not replaced between tests and may have been subject to fatigue as tests continued.

Correlating observed damages to measured pressure represented a key piece of the objectives in this work. As such, presenting the pressure as an average defined by all sensors was deemed more appropriate than defining damage by a pressure measurement taken from a single location. The 5kHz pressure data was first smoothed with a 21-point centered moving average. The smoothed pressure data is presented throughout for analysis and discussion. The 95% confidence interval (
U
95
) of the data was then calculated from the variance of all sensors inside the compartment during pressure wave loading before garage door damage or ejection. The uncertainty bounds were calculated as
U
95
= ± 1 . 96 ⋅
σ
p
,
σ
p
=
∑
i = 1
n
( P
( t )
−
P
̄
)
2
/ n − 1
. Here,
U
95
is the 95% confidence interval and
σ
p
is the standard deviation in pressure
P
( t )
over n number of samples. For an example case, all pressure traces, the average, and the
U
95
for one test are shown in Fig. A.15. For further analysis, the average of the pressure channels is used, with error regions defined by the
U
95
bounds. A cubic smoothing spline was fit to the average of all channels then integrated over the positive portion of pressure data to obtain an impulse ( I ) measurement for each case.

Fig. 8 shows the peak overpressure and impulse for all NCA and LFP bag experiments. Error bars represent the 95% confidence interval of the maximum value of overpressure or impulse as determined by all sensors. The horizontal damage lines are correlated to overpressure measurements and were determined by correlating pressure data with damage observations.

The ‘door damage begins’ threshold was defined by the 165L NCA bag experiment. In this experiment, the bottom of the garage door was deformed outward in the center, approximately 5cm. The line denoting ‘major door damage’ was defined by the 330L NCA bag experiment. In this test, the garage door panels began separating at their joints, and the bottom center of the door was deformed by approximately 15cm. The final line, denoting ‘garage door ejection’, was defined by the 320L LFP bag experiment. In this test, the garage door was pushed off of its track in one corner, constituting the beginning of a complete failure and ejection hazard; this is also the first case shown in Fig. 7.

Fig. 9 shows the peak overpressure versus impulse (P-I) for all gas bag experiments, along with several threshold curves. The data used to construct these threshold curves was obtained from Baker et al. [13] which reviewed explosion data from Jarrett [14]. Baker notes that these original thresholds were created from data on brick homes but can be interpreted for homes, small offices, and light-framed industrial buildings of similar construction. Jarret [14] defined minor structural damage as broken windows, fallen ceilings, joinery moved from fixing points, and loosened wall slates. The threshold for major damage is defined by uninhabitable structures with partial exterior wall demolition of one or two walls and severe damage to load-bearing partitions.

Significant damage was observed with overpressure and impulse below the original Jarrett [14] and Baker [13] thresholds for minor structural damage. Cracked wooden wall studs, dislodged drywall, deformed man-door latch plates, and ejected garage doors were observed during the 826L NCA, 1651L NCA, and 320L LFP gas bag experiments. The 826L NCA and 320L LFP gas bag experiments achieved peak internal pressures of 2.79kPa and 2.51kPa, respectively. In both cases, the maximum pressure was approximately 50% less than the threshold established by Jarrett or Baker. In the dispersion cases, the 10 008L NCA gas release obtained a maximum overpressure of 1.11kPa (75% below) and impulse of 205kPa ⋅ ms. The LFP 6809L release obtained a maximum overpressure of 1.75kPa (61% below) and impulse of 368kPa ⋅ ms.

Based on these observed damages in Fig. 7, two new P-I correlation lines were added to the original relations established by Baker et al. [13]. The first line, ‘fire protection envelope’, was defined by initial ejection of the garage door, deformation to door strike plates, drywall displacement of more than 1cm, and drywall cracking. The second line is a re-positioning of Baker’s ‘minor structural damage’ line, and is defined by the deformation or cracking of wooden structural members below the original threshold established in Baker. Further visualization of this damage can be seen in Fig. 7, where the first damage threshold would begin between 330L and 826L of NCA gas, and the second damage threshold begins between 826L and 1651L of NCA gas in the bag cases. Lower gas volumes still caused minor damage to the structure, including partial deformation of the garage door or damage to the man-door strike plate. The adjusted P-I lines were created using the location-averaged peak values of impulse and overpressure for the 330L and 826L NCA cases, respectively.

The added threshold for damage to the fire protection envelope is lower than the proposed damage thresholds by Baker et al. [13]. Data from experiments suggests that a modern garage structure has a lower enclosure strength, particularly considering damage to wooden framing members being above the minor damage threshold established by Jarrett [14]. Additionally, damage to the roll-up garage door and fire protection envelope occurs at significantly lower thresholds than other types of damage but is considered consequential. Baker and Mueschke both acknowledge that these thresholds are to be used as a representative reference since the P-I curve for any specific structure will vary with construction type, positioning, and various other factors [12,13]. Mannan [43] also notes similar thresholds for brick wall panels (20.7kPa–68.9kPa) and standard house construction (6.89kPa–13.8kPa) as Baker; values which are significantly lower than peak values measured in these experiments [43]. Although specific to the structure tested, experimental observations suggest that damage thresholds for modern lightweight construction are likely lower than for the brick structures studied in works such as Jarrett [14] and Baker [13].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison to gas release from LiB products
ce:section.ce:para:
Also shown in Fig. 9, experimental volumes of gas were equated to potential gas release volumes from LiB-powered devices. Linear correlations between gas volume and measurements of maximum overpressure and impulse were developed for data from NCA and LFP gas bag experiments. For the NCA experiments, maximum overpressure and impulse were linearly correlated to gas volume by factors of 0.0034kPa ⋅ L−1 (
R
2
= 0.981) and 0.1646kPa ⋅ ms ⋅ L−1 (
R
2
= 0.985), respectively. For the LFP experiments the factors were calculated to be 0.0046kPa ⋅ L−1 for maximum overpressure (
R
2
= 0.984) and 0.1535kPa ⋅ ms ⋅ L−1 for impulse (
R
2
= 0.919). These correlations are expressed in Fig. 9 as the black dashed (NCA) and black dotted (LFP) lines. The LFP surrogate gas yielded greater maximum pressure and impulse per liter than the NCA surrogate gas. This is expected due to the higher burning velocity of the gas. Literature also indicates that LFP Li-ion batteries typically produce less volume of TREG per watt-hour [22,23,44,45].

Literature studies examining gaseous products of battery thermal runaway typically give a range of liters of gas released per unit of electrical energy capacity watt-hour (W h) or ampere-hour capacity (A h) for different cathode chemistries. For this work, an analysis range of 0.44L ⋅ W−1
⋅ h−1 to 0.76L ⋅ W−1
⋅ h−1 is used, as obtained from Hoelle et al. [44] for NCA cathode chemistry. Extrapolation of data from a study by Golubkov et al. [45] found a similar range for NCA cells of 0.38L ⋅ W−1
⋅ h−1 to 0.61L ⋅ W−1
⋅ h−1. Data from Shen et al. determined a value of 0.18L ⋅ W−1
⋅ h−1 for LFP, while Yuan et al. data correlated to a value of 0.27L ⋅ W−1
⋅ h−1 for LFP [22,46]. The values for LFP are not used in the analysis to calculate a range of gas release volumes due to the limited number of LFP gas bag experiments; the trendline is still shown in Fig. 9.

Three sizes of battery were selected for comparison to the experimental data in this study: (1) a small-sized battery such as one used in commercially available electric lawn mowers, assumed to be 200W ⋅ h in size; (2) a medium-sized battery typically found in e-mobility devices, assumed to be 3300W ⋅ h; and (3) a large-sized battery used in EVs, assumed to be 80 500W ⋅ h. Ranges of gas production for each proposed device were calculated by multiplying the battery capacity in W h by the minimum and maximum liters of gas produced per unit of electrical energy capacity obtained from the literature, then by the overpressure and impulse per liter obtained experimentally. These ranges of overpressure and impulse are shown in Fig. 9 and are labeled as: 200W ⋅ h, 3300W ⋅ h and 80 500W ⋅ h. This way, the approximate consequences of a TREG release and delayed ignition are estimated for different battery sizes. However, it should be noted that the amount and composition of TREG are highly variable under different scales, SOCs, and battery chemistries. An analysis such as this will increase in utility and benefit from improvements in accuracy as research on LiB TREG volume and composition continues.

Fig. 10 shows the maximum internal compartment pressure obtained from bag experiment data compared with calculations from Jo and Park [16] and Ogle [15]. The lines in Fig. 10 represent model calculations for NCA gas, where the black circles and crosses represent LFP gas. An inset plot shows the LFP cases in more detail, as LFP was only investigated at small volumes ( ≤ 320L).

Both models calculate a linear increase in pressure with increasing flammable gas volume. Ogle displays closer agreement to experimental data for bag volumes 300L and less, while Jo and Park display closer agreement to experimental data for larger bag volumes. However, the largest tested bag volume of 3302L deviates from the model trend, having a maximum overpressure of 4.12kPa compared to 6.25kPa for the 1651L gas bag. This behavior may be attributed to the low-strength garage door, which relieved pressure and vented the still-combusting gas cloud, similar to a deflagration panel. These vented flammable gases then did not contribute to a proportional increase in pressure. For the 1651L NCA gas bag experiment, the garage door was ejected close to the moment of peak internal enclosure pressure, leaving a smaller amount of unburned gas in the enclosure at the time of venting compared to the 3302L NCA gas bag experiment. This also explains the greater impulse observed in this test. The decrease in maximum overpressure is attributed to uncertainty surrounding the strength of the garage door. Although the garage door is consistently the weakest point in the structural assembly, as it is not designed as such, there is uncertainty around its absolute strength regarding overpressure tolerance.

Only cases where the garage door was ejected are considered for comparison to the Mulpuru vented explosion model. These cases present a well-defined pressure rise and moment of maximum pressure, up to which the model can be compared. The cases examined are emphasized in Table 2. Fig. 11 shows the measured garage overpressure compared to predictions from the Mulpuru model for the 1651L and 3302L NCA gas bag experiments. The root mean squared error (RMSE) is used to evaluate the agreement between the model and experimental results and is calculated as RMSE
=
∑
i = 1
n
(
P
̄
−
P
ˆ
)
2
/ n
, where
P
ˆ
is the predicted pressure and
P
̄
is the average measured pressure over n samples. The 1651L case shows good agreement between the experimental data and model results, with the model slightly overpredicting pressure rise. RMSE between experimental average and model of this case is 1.993kPa. In the 3302L case, the rate of pressure rise is comparable between the experiment and model predictions with an RMSE of 0.910kPa. Fig. 11 also shows a similar comparison for the 320L case with LFP synTREG. For this case, the model slightly over-predicts the rate of pressure rise with an RMSE value of 0.904kPa.

In each bag case considered, the model over-predicted the rate of pressure rise when compared to experimental results. This demonstrates that the Mulpuru model may provide more conservative estimates of the rate of pressure rise for explosion hazard design calculations. Agreement between model and results when considering the bag experiments also benefits from the relatively small volumes of flammable gas, minimizing the error introduced by the constant flame speed assumption. However, this approach does not account for the remaining portions of explosion pressure time history, namely maximum internal pressure, subsequent pressure drop, and the effect of relief vent activation. Damage thresholds to structures rely on both maximum pressure and impulse, the latter of which would require the duration and magnitude of the entire pressure pulse to be calculated accurately.

Analysis of the dispersion experiments assumed the fuel occupied a spherical volume, the radius of which was defined by the assumed fuel concentration. Measurements from the portable methane gas detectors were used to determine the flammable gas concentration at the time of ignition.

Fig. 12a shows three methane gas meter measurements (extrapolated to total flammable gas concentration) as well as pressure versus time measurements for the 10 008L NCA gas dispersion experiment. Gas concentrations steadily increased over 20min as gas was added to the compartment. Pressure measurements (solid black line) and Mulpuru model calculations are shown on Fig. 12b. The red-shaded region is the predicted pressure based on the minimum (0.59) and maximum (0.66) equivalence ratios obtained from the portable gas detector data at ignition. The black dotted line is the predicted pressure obtained from the average measured equivalence ratio at ignition (0.63), corresponding to a sphere radius of 2.29m. The model over-predicted the rate of pressure rise in the analyzed NCA dispersion case, similar to the bag experiments examined. The RMSE for the 10 008L case was 0.110kPa when the Mulpuru model was evaluated using the average measured gas concentration at the time of ignition.

The optimal value of the equivalence ratio to minimize the error between the measurements and model predictions was determined by least squares regression to be 0.55. This value produces a flammable mixture sphere size of 2.38m at ignition and is presented as a dashed green line in Fig. 12b. The coefficient of determination of this fit was 0.998. This result indicates that the effective equivalence ratio at ignition may have been less than the values measured by the methane gas sensors. Adjusting the equivalence ratio for best fit, the RMSE improved to 0.024kPa. RMSE values comparing the experimental confidence interval (
U
95
) to the model are available in Table 3.

Fig. 13 shows the same analysis for the LFP dispersion experiment, during which 6809L of gas was released. In this case, only two portable gas detectors provided reliable data for calculating the minimum, maximum, and average equivalence ratios. The predicted pressure is again obtained from the average measured equivalence ratio (0.52) with a resulting sphere radius of 2.29m. The best-fit value of equivalence ratio was determined to be 0.64, corresponding to a sphere with radius 2.16m, with a coefficient of determination of 0.997. The RMSE for the 6809L case model was 0.095kPa evaluated using the average measured gas concentration at ignition from the ATO ( φ
=
0.52). The best fit RMSE improved to 0.037kPa. Additional values for RMSE comparing the experimental confidence interval (
U
95
) to the model are available in Table 3. From this result, the equivalence ratio at ignition was likely greater than the values measured in the compartment.

In contrast to the NCA dispersion case, the LFP dispersion case under-predicted the rate of pressure rise, likely due to several factors. One of the model assumptions used is constant flame speed, an assumption that does not hold as flammable volume increases. Turbulence also plays a significant role in changing the flame speed and pressure rise throughout fireball growth. Fireball growth is also assumed to be spherical and uniform. This assumption holds in the bag cases where, in the early stages, the flame is uniform and spherical. However, this assumption breaks down over time in the dispersion cases. In these cases, differences in flame propagation behavior could lead to differences in flame volume growth.

Table 3 summarizes the RMSE for the experimental cases evaluated against the model. For the bag cases, the RMSE label ‘avg.’ represents the average experimental data compared with the model, and the
U
9
5
+
and
U
9
5
−
represent the upper and lower 95% confidence interval bounds compared with the model. These error bounds provide a review of variations in experimental measurement propagating into comparisons with model results. For the dispersion cases the first set of RMSE values labeled ‘ATO avg.’, are the average and 95% confidence bounds of the experimental data compared to the model developed from the ATO measured fuel concentrations. This ATO measurement also has bounds (the red shaded areas on Figs. 12b& 13b). The error of these bounds is not evaluated. In the second set labeled ‘closest fit’, the average and 95% confidence bounds of the experimental data are compared to the best fit modeled data.

As a final step in analysis the Mulpuru model was used to evaluate the volume of burned gas at which the garage door is expected to fail. This is done as a further investigation of the ability of the Mulpuru model to predict volumes of flammable gas that would constitute a hazard. Based on experimental observation, it was determined that garage door failure occurred between the 320L LFP bag and 826L NCA bag, corresponding to between 2.5kPa and 2.8kPa. A similar analysis by Archibald [47] used a reference value of 1.18kPa for garage door failure, obtained from Jaffe et al. [48] who tested garage doors against constant wind speed and obtained values of 0.56kPa to 1.75kPa. Additionally, with the video analysis method detailed in Section 3.3, experimental values for burned gas volume were obtained for the 165L and 330L NCA gas bag experiments. These time-resolved measurements of burned gas are compared to the Mulpuru modeled values in Fig. 14a.

The Mulpuru model was then evaluated for the 1651L and 3302L NCA gas bag experiments, during which the door was ejected from the garage. Fig. 14b shows the predicted overpressure versus burned volume for these experiments, describing the volume of burned gas required to generate an overpressure corresponding to garage door ejection. It was found that garage door ejection (occurring between 2.5kPa and 2.8kPa) is expected to coincide with 0.14m3 to 0.17m3 (140L to 170L) of NCA gas mixed with air for the higher pressure case, and 0.30m3 to 0.35m3 (300L to 350L) for the lower pressure case. These volumes correspond to 52.50L to 63.75L of NCA gas for the lower calculation and 112.5L to 131.3L of NCA gas for the higher calculation. Experimental observation concluded that garage door ejection occurred between bag sizes of 330L and 826L of NCA gas mixture (124L to 310L of NCA synTREG), with the threshold on Fig. 8 defined by the 320L LFP bag experiment. Comparing the Mulpuru model prediction to the experiments, the volume of gas required to eject the garage door ranged from 0.94times to 5.9times the predicted value. In the case of these experiments, using the Mulpuru model would have provided conservative estimates for assessing the explosion hazard of accumulated TREG in a garage space.

This work examined two compositions of synthesized LiB thermal runaway effluent gases (synTREG) in confined (gas bag) and dispersed volumes. Experiments took place inside of a garage enclosure constructed to modern North American home standards.

Results demonstrated that small volumes of synTREG presented compelling explosion hazards. Experiments with gas mixtures in plastic bags conservatively demonstrated that a partial volume deflagration with between 124L and 310L of NCA synTREG (
V
b a g
=
330L and 826L), or approximately 91L of LFP synTREG (
V
b a g
=
320L), produced enough pressure to eject the two-car garage door. These synTREG volumes correspond to 1–2.5% of the enclosure volume LFL of the flammable gas composition, where 25% of the well-mixed LFL is used when assessing the precipitation of an explosion hazard. Correlations of cell gas volume production from the literature indicate that gas volumes of this size can be produced by battery packs commonly found in residential products, including RESS, large battery-powered tools, and e-mobility devices.

Peak internal compartment overpressures were less than 6.5kPa in all cases. These values are below published values for eardrum rupture thresholds (approximately 24kPa to 34kPa per Baker et al. [13]). Due to its low failure pressure, the garage door acted as a deflagration vent in multiple tests. The typical failure points of the door were the roller pin attachements to the garage door body. Ejection of the garage door may present a hazard to any personnel standing in its path. Projectiles besides the door were not observed in these experiments.

It was demonstrated that existing adiabatic mixing explosion models can conservatively predict the pressure rise before venting inside of an enclosure for an LiB synTREG deflagration. However, the models’ accuracy is limited as they do not account for structural response or venting. Models examining the pressure rise with time agreed well with experimental data in the bag cases, further supporting their use in evaluating pressure rise with time for TREG events. RMSE values evaluating model to averaged experimental measurement agreement ranged from 0.024kPa to 0.910kPa, with one experiment exceeding this range to a value of 1.993kPa. One limitation of the methodology being applied to the dispersion experiments was uncertainty in gas cloud size and fuel concentrations. Additionally, in other applications of Mulpuru a correlation is used to model flame speed with changing temperature and pressure. A constant flame speed assumption was used in this work, as it proved difficult to model this thermodynamic behavior with the multi-component synTREG mixture.

Overpressure measurements and damage observations revealed that existing pressure-impulse damage correlations can benefit from new data regarding the enclosure strengths of modern construction. Both structural damage and damage to the fire protection envelope of the garage enclosure were observed below the P-I thresholds reported in the literature. Li-ion thermal runaway presents a likely succession of explosion hazard followed by compartment fire. Therefore, the impact of a deflagration on the structure’s ability to contain a fire that will likely follow is important for understanding the life safety hazard. In this case, failure of building envelope components would enhance vulnerability to fire spread into interstitial spaces and adjacent structures.
